content.full_text:
California Association of Bond Lawyers
"CABL"
(Pronounced "Cable")

Executive Committee                          611 Anton Boulevard, Suite 1400
                                            P.O. Box 1950
Stan Wolcott                                Costa Mesa, California 92628-1950
President                                  714/641-3421, FAX 714/546-9035

Arto C. Becker
Vice President                             August 27, 1992

David McEwen
Vice President

William Kadi
Secretary/Treasurer

Board of Directors

Sydney B. Bennion

P. Addison Covert

Jeffrey P. Christopher

Jerrome N. Duncan II


Mr. Wayne Ordos
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Ordos:

     I would like to take this opportunity to belatedly thank you
for meeting briefly with Dave McEwen, Lyle Defenbaugh and myself on
August 10. At your invitation, I would like to express CABL's
concern over the effect of two recent informal assistance letters
issued by the FPPC.

     The first letter is Informal Assistance Letter No. I-92-049
dated May 20, 1992 and addressed to Robert Boehm (the "Chico
Letter"). In this letter, the FPPC concluded that a person who
contracts with a public agency to provide legal services as bond
counsel is a consultant for purposes of the Act. The second letter
is Informal Assistance Letter No. I-90-686 dated February 8, 1991
and addressed to Luis A. Rodriguez (the "Orange Letter"). In this
letter, the FPPC concluded that the attorney "consultant" must
disqualify himself from participating in any decision which affects
his firm.

     While we do not fully agree with the analysis contained in the
Chico Letter, we do recognize circumstances under which "bond
counsel" should be considered consultants and thereby responsible
for disclosing financial interests. The problem is with the

FS2\140\0150\93-000120\0049, 08/27/92

This image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with a light cream/beige background. There is no visible text content to transcribe. The page shows some faint horizontal lines that may be fold marks or page divisions, but no readable text is present.

California Association of Bond Lawyers

August 27, 1992
Page 2

consultant regulations which provides that a consultant must be a
"natural person". This ignores the fact that legal services are
provided pursuant to a contract between the firm which employs the
attorney and the public agency. Under the regulations, the
attorney providing the services and not the firm would be
considered the consultant. However, payment for services is made
directly to the firm and not the "consultant".

As the Orange Letter advises, the "consultant" lawyer would be
required to disqualify himself from participating in any decision
which would materially affect the firm (his source of income). Of
course, if the lawyer fails to participate in the decision as he is
required to do under the terms of his engagement, his firm would
not be entitled to any fee because no services would have been
rendered. The result of this analysis is that public agencies
would be prevented from receiving the services for which they have
contracted or they would be forced to contract with a multiplicity
of individual attorneys, the very thing which they sought to avoid
by engaging the firm as "general counsel". This, in our view, was
not the intended purpose of the Act. (Cf. Ritchie Advice Letter
dated March 19, 1979). The Orange Letter also suffers from its
failure to take into account the provisions of Section 18700(d)(3)
of the FPPC Regulations.

We understand that the FPPC is considering adopting
regulations concerning this entire area and we would be happy to
make ourselves available to assist you in their formulation. Given
the disruption which these letters have caused in the municipal
finance industry, I hope that the promulgation of these regulations
may proceed on an urgency basis. In the interim, we would suggest
that both the Chico Letter and Orange Letter be withdrawn in order
to eliminate the turmoil which they have brought about.

Very truly yours,

Stan Wolcott,
President

cc: Board of Directors
    Lyle Defenbaugh




FS2\146\0\5093-0001\201\0049. (08/27/92)
